coredata.dc:title: Preparation of blue fluorescent copper nanoclusters for sensitive and selective sensing of apigenin in pharmaceutical samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhifeng
Zhang, Yi
Jin, Meiling
Hao, Meiling
Yang, Huijuan
Peng, Yang
Lu, Jingwen
Zhang, Yaofang
Dong, Jiaxu
Ren, Jiali
Zhang, Ran
Wang, Ya
coredata.dc:description: One-pot means was performed for the rapid preparation of copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs), which were employed as a fluorescence system for the sensitive apigenin measurement in pharmaceutical samples. Herein, CuCl2 aqueous solution was reduced to Cu NCs through ascorbic acid and the Cu NCs were protected through trypsin under 65 ℃ for 4 h. The entire preparation process was rapid, facile and environmentally friendly. The trypsin-capped Cu NCs were demonstrated through ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime, respectively. The Cu NCs revealed blue fluorescence with emission wavelength around 465 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The fluorescence weakening feature of Cu NCs with apigenin was observed. On this basis, a facile and sensitive turn-off fluorescent nanoprobe for the sensing of apigenin in real samples was developed. The logarithm of relative fluorescence intensity revealed a good linear relationship with apigenin contents from 0.5 μM to 300 μM with the detection limit of 0.079 μM. The Cu NCs-based fluorescent nanosensor have been employed to measure the apigenin amounts in real samples such as medical saline, bovine and human serum. The results revealed excellent potential of this Cu NCs-based fluorescent nanoprobe for the convention computation of apigenin amounts in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Apigenin
Real samples

Process for apigenin measurement in real samples
Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor for the apigenin measurement
Selectivity of Cu NCs as fluorescent nanosensor for apigenin determination
Measurement of apigenin in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV spectra of Cu NCs, trypsin, CuCl2 and ascorbic acid; (B) Excitation and emission spectra of Cu NCs (inset: the photo of Cu NCs); (C) Excitation spectra under different emission wavelength; (D) FT-IR spectrum of Cu NCs.
(A) TEM image of Cu NCs; (B) XPS spectrum of Cu NCs; (C) Cu2p spectrum; (D) C1s spectrum; (E) N1s spectrum; (F) O1s spectrum.
(A) The emission spectra of detection system with different apigenin concentration; (B) The linear curve between ln(F0/F) and apigenin concentration (0.5–300 μM).
(A) The selectivity of Cu NCs on apigenin over other controls; (B) Interference of the Cu NCs for apigenin(the apigenin concentration was 300 μM, other control concentration was 1.5 × 104 μM).
(A) UV spectrum of apigenin, excitation and emission spectra of Cu NCs; (B) Lifetime of Cu NCs without and with apigenin.
Illustration of the formation process of Cu NCs and their function application.
Comparison of merits methods for apigenin measurement.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: Capillary electrophoresis with electrochemical detection (CE-ED); activated screen printed carbon electrode (ACE); N-carbon dots (N-CDs).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The apigenin detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation of blue fluorescent copper nanoclusters for sensitive and selective sensing of apigenin in pharmaceutical samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, P. R. of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, P. R. of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-pot means was performed for the rapid preparation of copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs), which were employed as a fluorescence system for the sensitive apigenin measurement in pharmaceutical samples. Herein, CuCl2 aqueous solution was reduced to Cu NCs through ascorbic acid and the Cu NCs were protected through trypsin under 65 ℃ for 4 h. The entire preparation process was rapid, facile and environmentally friendly. The trypsin-capped Cu NCs were demonstrated through ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime, respectively. The Cu NCs revealed blue fluorescence with emission wavelength around 465 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The fluorescence weakening feature of Cu NCs with apigenin was observed. On this basis, a facile and sensitive turn-off fluorescent nanoprobe for the sensing of apigenin in real samples was developed. The logarithm of relative fluorescence intensity revealed a good linear relationship with apigenin contents from 0.5 μM to 300 μM with the detection limit of 0.079 μM. The Cu NCs-based fluorescent nanosensor have been employed to measure the apigenin amounts in real samples such as medical saline, bovine and human serum. The results revealed excellent potential of this Cu NCs-based fluorescent nanoprobe for the convention computation of apigenin amounts in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Apigenin (5,7-Dihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4H-1-benzopyran-4-one, Fig. S1), a member of the flavonoids, is widely distributed in nature [1,2]. It mainly exists in Periaceae, Verbena, Selaginaceae plants, vegetables and fruits in warm tropics, especially in celery [3,4]. According to a large number of studies at home and abroad, apigenin has various biological activities such as anti-tumor, cardiovascular and cerebrovascular protection, anti-virus, and anti-bacterial [5–7]. Due to the importance of apigenin for human health, the determination of apigenin content in biological fluids is of great importance in different fields. To date, different technologies have been exploited to measure apigenin including included high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8–13], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [14–17], LC-MS/MS [18], MS [19,20], electrochemical method [21], capillary electrophoresis with electrochemical detection (CE-ED) [22] and fluorescent method [23]. Except for fluorescent method, other traditional methods are limited due to their sophisticated operation procedure, complicated synthesis, complex labeling procedure, high cost and more time consumption. The fluorescent methods are very prospective because of their good points of austerity, selectivity, sensitivity and lower cost of instruments. The core of fluorescence methods were the preparation of fluorescent materials. Fluorescent materials mainly included semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, metal organic skeleton structures and metal nanoclusters (copper nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters, gold nanoclusters and platinum nanoclusters). Especially, fluorescent copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) have suggested extensive interest due to its distinctive physical and chemical features including fluorescence, solubility, biocompatibility and easily usable metal ions for the development of Cu NCs as compared to the high cost metal ions for Au NC, Ag NCs and Pt NCs [24–26]. Until now, different Cu NCs have been carried out as fluorescent nanosensors to measure various substances, such as Cu2+ [27], Pb2+ [28], Al3+ [29], Fe3+ [30], Ag+ [31], Hg2+ [32], H2O2 [33], ClO- [34], graphene oxide [35], 2,4,6-trinitrophenol [36], 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene [37], glucose [38], alkaline phosphatase activity [39], histidine [40], ascorbic acid [41] and nitrite [42].However, as far as we know, the Cu NCs-based fluorescent nanosensors have not been used to measure apigenin.

To develop fluorescent Cu NCs, different protecting ligands, including amino acids, polymers, proteins, DNA and sulfhydryl compounds, have been carried out [43–45]. At the same time, various methods, such as microwave irradiation, sonochemical, electrochemical method, microemulsion method and chemical reduction method, have been applied for the development of fluorescent Cu NCs [46–48]. For example, the tannic acid-stabilized copper nanoclusters were successfully conducted though tannic acid as the stabilizer and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent, which displayed blue fluorescence at the emission wavelength of 434 nm. Moreover, the detection limit was 0.12 μM and linear relationship existed in the range of 0.2–100 μM [49]. Chauhan et al. [50] developed red-emitting BSA stabilized copper nanoclusters, which were applied to detect vitamin B6 cofactors and nitroaromatics due to the energy and electron transfer processes. Wang’s group carried out glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters for sensitive and selective determination of p-nitrophenol and alkaline phosphatase activity based on the inner filter effect and the limit of detection of p-nitrophenol and alkaline phosphatase activity were 20 nM and 0.003 mU·mL−1 [51].Thus, the establishment of simple, fast, and friendly ways to prepare fluorescent Cu NCs was still necessary. Moreover, none of these Cu NCs protected with proteins have been employed to measure apigenin to date.

In this work, a green and sample method for one-step development of Cu NCs was prepared through trypsin as the template and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. The as-developed Cu NCs were excellent water solubility and revealed blue fluorescence with the emission wavelength of 465 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Additionally, when apigenin was introduced into the Cu NCs solution, the fluorescent intensities decreased, which was assumed to the inner filter effect, and the logarithm of F0/F was linearly performed on the apigenin contents. In this aspect, a sensitive and selective method for apigenin detection was carried out. As far as we know, this was the first work to develop fluorescent method for the sensing of apigenin based on Cu NCs.Scheme 1

Cupric chloride, trypsin and ascorbic acid were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. Sodium sulfate, sodium nitrate, lysine, cysteine, glutathione, human serum albumin, citric acid, sodium citrate, tryptophan, methionine, histidine, glutamate, alanine, proline, aspartic acid, leucine, phenylalanine, arginine, tyrosine, serine and apigenin were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were of reagent grade and used without further purifications.

The fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) data of Cu NCs were explored through an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a U-4100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The particle size measurements of Cu NCs were carried out on a Tecnai G2 20 electron microscope (FEI, USA) with the voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi) was exploited to discuss the elemental composition and valence of Cu NCs. The functional groups on the surface of Cu NCs were studied by using a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tenson 27, Bruker, Germany). The lifetime of Cu NCs was investigated on a FLS1000 (Edinburgh, Scotland).

For development of Cu NCs, trypsin solution (10 mL, 40 mg/mL) was prepared at room temperature. Then, the trypsin solution (10 mL) and CuCl2 (10 mL, 0.01 M) were mixed by stirring for 2 min. The ascorbic acid solution (1 mL, 0.1 M) was dripped in trypsin and CuCl2 solution. The pH value of mixture was adjusted to 5.2 by hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solution and reacted for 4 h for 65 ℃. The light blue solution gradually turned to light yellow. Furthermore, the Cu NCs were purified through dialysis bag (MW: 4500 Da). Finally, the Cu NCs were stored in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General process for apigenin measurement using Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the Cu NCs solution (1 mL) were introduced into various apigenin contents from 0 μM to 300 μM with citric acid and sodium citrate buffer solution (1 mL, pH = 6). After reaction 100 s, the fluorescent intensities of the above system around 465 nm were analyzed under excitation wavelength of 380 nm at room temperature. The fluorescent analysis were carried out for three times, and the average values were obtained via calculation. To examine the influences of controls, the fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs with different controls such as SO4 2-, NO3 –, cysteine, glutathione, human serum albumin, lysine, tryptophan, methionine, histidine, glutamate, alanine, proline, aspartic acid, leucine, phenylalanine, arginine, tyrosine and serine were carried out to investigate the selectivity of Cu NCs as fluorescent nanosensor for apigenin determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process for apigenin measurement in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The bovine serum samples were received from Kelong Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. (Chengdu, China). The medical saline and human serum samples were acquired from the clinic. The samples were processed through filtration and dilution with PBS solution. The apigenin solution were added into the samples, and the all fluorescence data were acquired though fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The Quantum yield (QY) value of the Cu NCs was investigated based on the reported literature [24]. The calculation process of QY value was based on formula.
Φ =
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
× Φ R
where Φ represents the quantum yield of Cu NCs, I refers to the integral area of fluorescent spectrum (at 465 nm), η represents the refractive index of solvent, A represents the UV–vis absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. R is the quinine sulfate.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the acquired Cu NCs demanded a fresh absorption peak around 366 nm, which was different from CuCl2, trypsin and ascorbic acid. The apparent peaks of Cu NCs were not found with the wavelength varied from 400 to 600 nm, which was the proof of absence of large copper nanoparticles [52]. At the same time, the UV–vis absorption spectra indicated the preparation of Cu NCs. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength were suggested at 380 and 465 nm, respectively. The blue fluorescence and light yellow of Cu NCs were observed under UV light and sunlight (inset Fig. 1B). The effect of excitation wavelength on the emission wavelength were studied with varying excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 390 nm. As displayed in Fig. 1C, the emission wavelength did not have a fixed value under different excitation wavelength, revealing that this Cu NCs had an excitation-dependent behavior. And this behavior was related to the case of particle size distribution [53]. The quantum yield of Cu NCs was 4.23 %. The FT-IR spectrum of Cu NCs displayed the feature bands of trypsin as a protecting agent, indicating that the trypsin was introduced into the surface of Cu NCs. The peaks at 3378.2, 2977.3, 1656.3, 1261.1 and 1142.8 cm−1 were assumed to the stretching vibration of –OH, CH, CO, CN and CO bonds [54,55]. The peaks at 1426.5 and 1383.4 cm−1 were ascribed to asymmetric and symmetrical deformation vibration of CH bond (Fig. 1D) [56].

Fig. 2 A demanded the Cu NCs’s TEM image. It could be seen, the shape and average particle size of Cu NCs were spherical and 2.34 nm. The elemental composition of Cu NCs was measured through XPS analysis. The four feature peaks around 285.4, 398.1, 531.9 and 933.2 eV were acquired by XPS full spectrum, which were ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p of C, N, O and Cu element, respectively (Fig. 2B) [57]. The C 1 s single spectrum carried out three peaks at 284.5, 285.9 and 287.5 eV, which were the peaks of CC, CN and CO (Fig. 2D) [58]. The N 1 s single spectrum displayed two peaks at 399.6 and 401.1 eV, which were the peaks of CNC and C-NH (Fig. 2E) [59]. The O 1 s single spectrum exhibited three peaks at 531.2, 532.2 and 532.5 eV, which were the peaks of CO, COC and C-OH (Fig. 2F) [60]. The two peaks around 932.6 and 952.4 eV standing for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 were observed (Fig. 2C) [61]. Furthermore, we did not discovery any characteristic peaks at 942 eV, revealing that the Cu2+ was reduced to C0 and Cu+ [62]. Meanwhile, from the results of XPS, the percentage of C, Cu, N and O were 51.09 %, 0.25 %, 20.93 % and 38.77 % in Cu NCs.

The as-obtained Cu NCs displayed excellent stability. In detail, after storage of 21 days at room temperature, the fluorescent intensities of Cu NCs had no conspicuous variety, suggesting that the Cu NCs possessed good stability (Fig. S2A). Additionally, although increasing of sodium chloride led to the fluorescence enhancement of Cu NCs, the fluorescent intensities of Cu NCs with NaCl-containing solutions displayed minor variety when the NaCl contents reached 0.6 M, suggesting that the Cu NCs had strong salt tolerance (Fig. S2B). As suggested in Fig. S2C, when the UV time was 5 min, the degree of weakening was very small, exhibiting the outstanding photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu NCs as fluorescent sensor for the apigenin measurement
Selectivity of Cu NCs as fluorescent nanosensor for apigenin determination
ce:section.ce:para: To perform the selectivity of Cu NCs as fluorescent nanoprobe for apigenin determination, the influences of different controls including lysine, tryptophan, methionine, histidine, glutamate, alanine, proline, aspartic acid, leucine, phenylalanine, arginine, tyrosine, serine, SO4 2-, NO3 –, cysteine, glutathione and human serum albumin on the fluorescent intensities of Cu NCs were discussed under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. As exhibited in Fig. 4 A, all controls, except apigenin, had no significant influences on the fluorescent reaction of Cu NCs even when the control contents reached 50 times than that of apigenin. In the case where apigenin and reference substances existed at the same time, the antiinterference tests were performed under the same conditions (Fig. 4B). This selective results revealed that the Cu NCs as an excellent nanoprobe for apigenin determination have great potential in real samples.

As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the UV–vis absorption feature peak of apigenin overlapped distinctly with the fluorescent excitation peak of Cu NCs. Based on this spectral overlapping phenomenon, the excitation energy of Cu NCs were occupied by apigenin, which the sensing mechanisms were presumed to be the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner-filter effect (IFE) [63].

For the occurrence of FRET, the fluorescence weakening and the decrease fluorescence lifetime of probes were caused by the excited state energy transfer of probes [64]. To study IFE, the fluorescence quenching could occur, while the lifetime remained almost unchanged. As suggested in Fig. 5B, after addition of apigenin, the fluorescence lifetime of Cu NCs had no change, revealing that the FRET could be ruled out (lifetime of Cu NCs was 3.79 ns, lifetime of Cu NCs with apigenin was 3.80 ns). Due to the discovery, IFE was very likely to be the quenching mechanism. To be our knowledge, the radiation energy transfer was caused by IFE. The IFE could be broken down into primary IFE and secondary IFE. The primary IFE and secondary IFE were caused by weakening of excitation radiation and re-absorption of emission radiation, respectively. Furthermore, the primary IFE should be the determination mechanism [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of apigenin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further discuss the application performance of this prepared system, the as-prepared Cu NCs as a fluorescent nanosensor was employed to measure apigenin in real samples, such as medical saline, bovine serum and human serum samples. With regards to this, the processed samples were utilized to measure apigenin following the programs in 2.5 part. The calculate data were displayed in Table 2 . The obtained results for apigenin measurement in real samples through this system were satisfactory according to the recoveries and relative standard deviation of 93.3–107.1 % and 2.47–3.45 %, respectively. Then, the above results indicated the good potential of Cu NCs-based nanosensor for the conventional computation of apigenin concentration in real samples.

In conclusion, a new and one-pot strategy was fabricated for the synthesis of Cu NCs-based fluorescent senor through trypsin as the protecting agent and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent at 65 ℃. The emission wavelength at 465 nm of Cu NCs was obtained under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescence feature was investigated via discussing the effect of excitation wavelength on emission wavelength. Inspired by apigenin-induced fluorescence emission decrease of Cu NCs, a novel and facile fluorescent platform was carried out to measure apigenin, which has been successfully applied for the measurement of apigenin in real samples. As far as we know, this as-suggested work was first sensing platform of a fluorescence nanoprobe for apigenin with linear range and LOD of 0.5–300 μM and 0.079 μM. This developed Cu NCs-based fluorescent system for apigenin determination was facile, rapid and efficient. Additionally, this system had great potential for the sensing of apigenin in the other fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (No. 20210302124210) and the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122940.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
